title: Tumor Cell Morphology
content: Malignant cells often show atypical morphology, including irregular nuclear size and shape, increased nucleus-to-cytoplasm ratio, abnormal membrane features, and loss of normal structural organization. Morphological atypia is useful as a first-pass indicator of abnormal cell state, but it should be interpreted as evidence of altered cellular organization rather than proof of a specific mutation.
limitations: This source is broad and oncology-oriented. It supports morphology-based anomaly explanation but should not be used to claim a specific diagnosis or molecular mechanism from shape alone.
follow_up: Use together with quantitative shape metrics and, when relevant, recommend molecular or histological follow-up rather than definitive mutation claims.

title: Tumor Cell Morphology
content: Abnormal membrane morphology, including atypical microvilli, pseudopod-like protrusions, and altered surface organization, can accompany invasive or dysregulated cell behavior. In a morphology-only system, these surface irregularities can be described as signs of structural abnormality or altered motility-related organization, not as direct evidence of metastasis or a defined molecular pathway.
limitations: Surface changes are biologically meaningful but still non-specific. Similar-looking protrusive or irregular patterns can arise from multiple contexts.
follow_up: Frame protrusions and irregular contours as possible indicators of altered structural or mechanical state and suggest confirmatory downstream analysis if needed.

title: 3D karyometry in preneoplastic progression
content: Quantitative 3D morphology can distinguish normal, metaplastic, and dysplastic cell states using features such as cell size, nuclear size, nucleus-to-cytoplasm ratio, deviation from sphericity, and internal structural heterogeneity. In an explanation layer, reduced regularity and stronger deviation from spherical organization can be described as signs of morphological abnormality rather than as standalone proof of malignancy.
limitations: The original study focuses strongly on nuclear and subnuclear descriptors. It supports 3D morphology interpretation but should be translated carefully when applied to whole-cell shape summaries.
follow_up: Use with cautionary wording such as 'associated with' or 'consistent with' rather than definitive clinical statements.

title: 3D karyometry in preneoplastic progression
content: Progression toward abnormal cell states can be reflected by larger size, greater structural irregularity, increased heterogeneity, and stronger departure from geometrically regular forms. These patterns are useful for describing why a reconstructed 3D cell looks atypical compared with a more regular reference morphology.
limitations: This is evidence for morphology-state association, not for cause, mutation, or treatment response.
follow_up: Best used to justify model explanations such as 'the cell appears less regular and more structurally heterogeneous than expected for a typical morphology.'

title: VAMPIRE morphology protocol
content: Eccentricity describes how strongly a shape departs from a circular or elliptical form. Solidity is the ratio between object area and its convex hull area and reflects how concave or indented a shape is. Roughness can be described as variability along the boundary relative to the centroid, which makes it useful for describing irregular surfaces and protrusive outlines.
limitations: These are geometric descriptors, not diagnoses. Their interpretation depends on biological context and imaging conditions.
follow_up: Use these definitions as authoritative support when explaining why a model flagged a cell as morphologically irregular.

title: VAMPIRE morphology protocol
content: Cell populations can contain substantial morphological heterogeneity even when simple scalar shape measures look similar. This means that multiple descriptors are often needed to explain abnormality, and a single metric alone may fail to capture the true complexity of cell shape.
limitations: This is especially important for irregular or protrusive shapes, where coarse descriptors can underrepresent local geometry.
follow_up: When generating LLM explanations, combine multiple features such as irregularity, roughness, convexity loss, and asymmetry instead of relying on one metric.

title: Substrate Properties Modulate Cell Membrane Roughness
content: Cell membrane roughness can reflect underlying cytoskeletal organization and mechanical state, but it is also influenced by environmental conditions such as substrate properties. Therefore, increased surface irregularity can be biologically meaningful while still remaining non-specific.
limitations: Roughness should not be treated as a disease-exclusive marker. It is safer to describe it as a morphology feature associated with altered structural state.
follow_up: Use roughness in a combined explanation with other geometric abnormalities instead of presenting it as a standalone determinant.

title: Cellular Morphological Features are Predictive Markers of Cancer Cell State
content: Cell morphology can function as a phenotypic signature of cell state. Shape, contour irregularity, and structural variation can carry predictive information about abnormal cellular behavior, but morphology alone does not uniquely identify the precise underlying mutation or molecular driver.
limitations: This is one of the key guardrail ideas for the project: morphology is informative, but not fully specific.
follow_up: Use this source to support explanations that describe anomaly as morphology-based evidence rather than as definitive molecular diagnosis.

title: Cellular Morphological Features are Predictive Markers of Cancer Cell State
content: Atypical morphology can be explained as a combination of shape irregularity, asymmetry, and altered structural organization relative to more regular reference cells. In a safe explanation pipeline, such a finding should be phrased as 'consistent with an abnormal cell state' rather than as proof of a specific mutation or treatment indication.
limitations: Do not escalate morphology-derived explanations into genotype-level claims.
follow_up: Prefer wording such as 'may indicate', 'can be associated with', and 'requires additional confirmation'.

title: Morphological Diversity of Cancer Cells Predicts Prognosis
content: Large-scale morphological diversity can correlate with clinically relevant biological severity, including instability and adverse prognosis. For a morphology-only explainer, this supports careful language such as 'greater morphological heterogeneity may be associated with a more abnormal or unstable cell state.'
limitations: Association with prognosis does not imply direct diagnosis in a single-sample morphology system.
follow_up: Use as background support for cautious implication language, not for patient-level prognostic claims.

title: A Pathologist's Journey from Morphology to Molecular
content: Morphology is a valuable starting point in pathology, but modern interpretation often requires molecular or complementary follow-up. A morphology-based anomaly flag should therefore be described as an initial structural finding that may justify further analysis rather than as a self-sufficient final conclusion.
limitations: This is the primary guardrail chunk for avoiding overclaiming in the RAG system.
follow_up: Good follow-up wording includes additional imaging, expert review, and molecular assays when clinically relevant.

title: CellProfiler Measurement Documentation
content: Convexity-related descriptors quantify how closely an object matches its convex hull, which helps describe indentations and non-convex boundaries. Eccentricity describes elongation or departure from roundness, and related shape measures support consistent morphology reporting across samples.
limitations: These definitions provide measurement meaning, not biological interpretation by themselves.
follow_up: Use these chunks as the authoritative layer underneath any LLM explanation of geometric metrics.

title: Cytologic Atypia of Benign Inflammatory vs Neoplastic Lesions
content: Features such as irregular nuclei, pleomorphic nuclei, altered nuclear envelopes, hyperchromasia, and peripheral nucleoli can help distinguish more abnormal from more benign cellular states. In a generalized morphology RAG base, this source is best used as a vocabulary source for atypia features rather than as a universal cross-tissue rulebook.
limitations: This paper is tissue-specific and should not be overgeneralized.
follow_up: Use only for supporting chunks about what atypia features can look like, not as the main decision logic.

title: Size and Shape Measurements
content: Shape metrics such as roundness, solidity, elongation, and eccentricity can be explained in plain language as ways of measuring how regular, indented, stretched, or uneven a cell appears. This source is useful for user-facing explanation text when paired with stronger scientific references.
limitations: Educational only; do not rely on it as the primary scientific authority.
follow_up: Use to simplify the final AI explanation for non-expert users.
